evidence: JSpecify @NonNull/@Nullable annotations used consistently on all public method parameters and return types across the codebase (QueryExecutor, SemanticFieldExplorer, ExplorationHandlers, CollocateQueryHelper, BlackLabSnippetParser, ExplorationService interface).

Records used for all data-carrying types: WordSketchResult, SnippetResult, CollocateResult, HitRecord, ExplorationOptions, SingleSeedExplorationOptions, RelationConfig — providing compile-time field-name and type guarantees.
ConcordanceResult is a sealed interface with `permits SnippetResult, CollocateResult`, enabling exhaustive pattern-matching and compiler-enforced hierarchy.

Optional used correctly for nullable lookup returns: GrammarConfig.relation() returns Optional<RelationConfig>; RelationConfig.relationType() returns Optional<RelationType>; never Optional.get() without a check.

All HTTP response bodies use Map<String, Object> — ubiquitous in ExploreResponseAssembler, ExplorationHandlers, SketchHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers. Typed model objects (ExplorationResult, DiscoveredNoun, etc.) exist upstream but are immediately flattened to untyped maps for JSON serialisation. The outbound API shape is structurally invisible to the compiler.

VisualizationHandlers defines a RenderMode enum with a single value SIGNED, but the mode flows downstream as a raw String (RadialPlot.renderFromItems signature: `String mode`). The enum validates presence/absence but is never used as a typed enum value — textbook enum bypass pattern.

RelationConfig.name() and RelationConfig.description() are both @Nullable. SketchHandlers calls obj.put("name", rel.name()) and obj.put("description", rel.description()) without null-guards in four separate call sites (lines 90, 91, 113, 218, 263, 264), while GrammarConfigSerializer.toJson guards both with `if (rel.name() != null)`. Inconsistent null-safety at the JSON boundary.

RelationType enum carries four legacy/dead values (ADJ_PREDICATE, ADJ_MODIFIER, SUBJECT_OF, OBJECT_OF) documented as 'Not set in grammar config'. They remain valid enum constants but no grammar config ever produces them. This creates a semantic mismatch: the type system permits these values in method signatures that conceptually exclude them.

CollocateQueryHelper no-arg constructor stores null in a BlackLabIndex field with no @Nullable annotation on the field. The design is intentional (testing seam) and documented, with Objects.requireNonNull guards in each method, but the field's declared type provides no nullability information to static analysis tools.

strengths: JSpecify @NonNull/@Nullable annotations applied systematically across all public method signatures and record fields — static analysis tools have meaningful signal throughout the codebase.
Records used for all data-carrying DTOs (WordSketchResult, HitRecord, RelationConfig, ExplorationOptions) giving compile-enforced field names and types with no setters.
ConcordanceResult sealed interface with explicit permits clause enables exhaustive pattern matching and compiler-enforced hierarchy integrity.
Optional used correctly for query-result accessors (GrammarConfig.relation, RelationConfig.relationType) with no raw Optional.get() calls observed without prior presence checks.
type_safety.issue_character: Issues split between a single systemic pattern (Map<String, Object> for all response body construction, spanning every handler class) and two isolated local lapses (RenderMode enum bypass, @Nullable field accessed without guard); the systemic issue is common in Java REST codebases but is a real type-discipline gap, not an oversight.
type_safety.score_rationale: The domain model layer is genuinely well-typed: records, sealed interfaces, JSpecify annotations, and correct Optional use place it well above the 60-range codebases that skip annotations or rely on runtime nullchecks. What pulls the score down from the 85+ tier is the universal collapse to Map<String, Object> at the response-assembly boundary — the compiler sees nothing of the JSON shape, and typed response objects could easily be defined for each endpoint. The RenderMode bypass and inconsistent nullable-field guards are smaller deductions. Overall this sits comfortably in the solid-but-uneven 70–75 band: strong on the input/model side, weak on the output side.

issues.summary: All HTTP response bodies built as Map<String, Object> despite typed upstream model objects existing
evidence: ExploreResponseAssembler.serializeEdge returns Map<String, Object> with keys 'source', 'target', 'log_dice', 'type' — shape is invisible to the compiler.
ExplorationHandlers.buildCoreExploreResponse constructs and returns Map<String, Object> with fields 'status', 'relation_type', 'parameters' inserted manually by string keys.
ExploreResponseAssembler.populateExploreResponse mutates a passed Map<String, Object> — callers cannot see which fields were set without reading the implementation.
CorpusQueryHandlers.buildBcqlResponse returns Map<String, Object> with 6 manually keyed fields despite the upstream BcqlRequest being a typed record.
issues.suggestion: Introduce typed response records (e.g. ExploreResponse, EdgeDto, SketchRelationResponse) for each endpoint family, serialise them with FastJSON or Jackson. This makes the outbound API shape compiler-checkable and removes the silent-typo risk in map key strings.

issues.summary: RenderMode enum defined but bypassed: mode flows to RadialPlot as raw String, never converted to enum type
evidence: VisualizationHandlers line 70: `if (mode != null && !mode.isEmpty() && !RenderMode.isValid(mode))` validates the string but never converts it.
RadialPlot.renderFromItems signature is `String mode` (line 145 of RadialPlot.java) — takes the raw string, so the enum provides no compile-time guarantee about what values are passed.
RenderMode has a single value SIGNED with a static isValid() string comparison method — pattern consistent with an enum that was added for type safety but whose type is not used downstream.
issues.suggestion: Change RadialPlot.renderFromItems to accept a @Nullable RenderMode parameter; convert the validated string to the enum before calling renderFromItems, or pass null when mode is absent. This makes the valid values compiler-enforced end-to-end.

issues.summary: RelationConfig.name() and description() are @Nullable but accessed without null-guard in SketchHandlers
evidence: RelationConfig record declares `@Nullable String name` and `@Nullable String description` (lines 23–24).
SketchHandlers.handleRelationsForType (lines 90–91): `obj.put("name", rel.name()); obj.put("description", rel.description())` — no null guards.
SketchHandlers.buildRelationResponse (lines 263–264): same unguarded access pattern for description.
GrammarConfigSerializer.toJson correctly guards both: `if (rel.name() != null) obj.put("name", rel.name())` — demonstrating the design intent but inconsistent application.
issues.suggestion: Either add null-guards in SketchHandlers to match GrammarConfigSerializer, or promote name/description to @NonNull with a validation step in GrammarConfigLoader (since missing names would likely be a config error). The inconsistency itself signals the annotation is not being enforced at call sites.

root_causes: The codebase invested in type discipline for the model/domain layer (records, JSpecify, sealed interfaces) but treated JSON response construction as presentation glue, leading to Map<String, Object> as a default response container rather than typed DTOs.
RenderMode was likely added to VisualizationHandlers as a validation step before typed rendering modes were needed; the RadialPlot API predates the enum and was never updated to accept the enum type.
likely_symptoms: nullable_relation_fields_unguarded — this is a call-site gap that depends on knowledge of @Nullable annotations; easy to miss in code review without static analysis enforcement.
